Specific activities vary from year to year depending on the interest of the students. Activities of the German Club include a fall Oktoberfest, a Christmas party, a Fasching or Karneval celebration, and German movie screenings and the bi-weekly Stammtisch meetings during the semester.
Membership in the club is open to all students interested in German language and culture.
summary: A special interest organization at the University of Alabama for students studying German or just interested in German culture!

name: Graduate History Association
description: The Graduate History Association (GHA) is an organization open to all M.A. and Ph.D. students in the University of Alabama history department. Its goal is to provide a way for new students to meet other students, to provide a social outlet for all members of the history department, and to sponsor events that prepare our members for aspects of the profession that they may not have been exposed to yet, such as conferences and publishing.
summary: The GHA exists to further the interests of history graduate students at the University of Alabama by providing educational, social, and networking opportunities.

name: Graduate Student Association
description:
Through representation, community, and support, GSA aims to serve all graduate students at the University of Alabama. Our Executive Council serves to undertake initiatives for the improvement of graduate student life with the help of committees and delegates from graduate programs and departments.

The mission of the Graduate Student Association is to serve the needs of, advocate for the interests of, and foster collaboration among graduate and professional students at The University of Alabama. As the advocate body for graduate students, the GSA helps graduate students voice their concerns to the larger University.

The GSA works with several departments and organizations on campus to provide services and support to graduate students. Some of our activities include: providing research and travel funding, partnering with the 3-Minute Thesis (3MT), and working with the SGA.

Every graduate student is a member of the GSA unless otherwise stated.
summary: Through representation, community, and support, GSA aims to serve all graduate students at the University of Alabama.

name: Hotel and Restaurant and Convention Association
description: HARACA aims to inspire and empower hospitality students at the University of Alabama by fostering connections with industry professionals, providing hands-on experiences, and cultivating a supportive community of future leaders in the hospitality industry.
summary: HARACA's purpose is to enrich our members by helping them experience various aspects of the Hospitality industry outside of the classroom.

name: Health Care Management and Analytics Society
description: We are a society devoted to introducing students to the wide span of health care related jobs and opportunities. We try to help students in finding their niche in health care, whether that be pharmaceutical sales, health insurance, hospital administration, etc.
summary: HCMAS is designed to connect students with opportunities to learn more about the health care fields. HCMAS provides students with opportunities for professional development and networking.

name: La Última Letra
description: The purposes of this association are to promote the professional development of graduate students in Spanish (linguistics and literature). We also are a social organization dedicated to enhancing camaraderie among graduate students and professors.
summary: Spanish, graduate students

name: Lady Tide Soccer
description:
Lady Tide Soccer, the University of Alabama's Women's Club Soccer Team, plays in the Southwest Collegiate Soccer Alliance (SCSA) against teams from universities across the south.

 

Our goal is to offer a competitive environment where girls who love soccer can continue their passion through college. In the fall we compete in SCSA Region II, playing about six games and one tournament traveling to different universities in the Southwest. Our intent in the fall is to compete to make it to Regionals at the end of the season, and then Nationals. In the spring we are more relaxed playing in tournaments and friendly, yet competitive games. We tend to focus more on fundraising and community service in the spring.

 

This student-run organization caters to those with a high level of play and prior experience. We are actively looking for new players to have a fun time and compete, so feel free to contact the officers listed below with any questions.

 

Tryouts are held after Get On Board Day in the fall, and informally in the spring. Follow us on Facebook at Lady Tide Club Soccer and on Instagram @ladytidesoccer to stay informed!
summary: Lady Tide Soccer, the University of Alabama's Women's Club Soccer Team, plays in the Southwest Collegiate Soccer Alliance (SCSA) against teams from universities across the south.

name: University Lutheran Church Student Ministry
description: General worship starts on Sundays at 10 AM, with Bible studies on Thursday evenings at 5:45 PM and dinner at 6:30. Join us for fellowship, weekly devotionals, and a monthly outing!
summary: We're a student community at University Lutheran Church. Join us on Sunday at 10 AM for worship or fellowship dinners at 6:30 PM on Thursday nights!

name: Alabama Interfraternity Council
summary: interfraternity, council

name: Kappa Kappa Gamma
description: Our Fraternity’s mission statement is, “Kappa Kappa Gamma is an organization of women, which seeks for every member throughout her life bonds of friendship, mutual support, opportunities for self-growth, respect for intellectual development, and an understanding of and an allegiance to positive ethical principles.”
summary: Kappa Kappa Gamma Sorority

name: Kappa Sigma
description: Kappa Sigma was established at the University of Alabama in 1867. Since then, the fraternity has pledged and initiated thousands of members while promoting the fraternity's ideals of leadership, scholarship, and service. Kappa Sigma is an IFC fraternity and participates in a variety of community service and philanthropy events throughout each semester.
summary: Since 1867, Kappa Sigma has worked to develop young men into leaders through the Four Pillars of fellowship, scholarship, leadership, and service.

name: Lambda Alpha Honor Society in Anthropology
description: The University of Alabama Beta chapter of Lambda Alpha is an honor society to promote excellence in Anthropology. Anthropology majors and minors (or anyone with at least 12 credit hours of anthropology classes) are invited to apply once per semester for the local chapter, and juniors, seniors, and graduate students may apply for the national chapter. There are scholarships available at the national level, and this is an excellent addition to graduate school applications and resumes! Both undergraduate students and graduate students can join!
summary: academic, honor society, anthropology

name: Lambda Chi Alpha
description: Founded in 1909, Lambda Chi Alpha has a mission to inspire the highest level of character, values, scholarship, and service in each of its members.
summary: lambda, chi, alpha

name: Lambda Sigma Honor Society
description: Lambda Sigma Honor Society is a national honor society for second year men and women dedicated to the purpose of fostering leadership, scholarship, fellowship, and the spirit of service among college students, and to promoting the interests of the college or university in every possible way. Membership in Lambda Sigma is not only recognition for accomplishment, but also a challenge to serve throughout your college career and as an alumnus. Lambda Sigma offers opportunities for personal growth, leadership experience, a sense of involvement on your campus, and the friendship of other student leaders/scholars. As a member of a group of outstanding second year students, you will be challenged to fulfill your own intellectual and leadership potential while serving your college/university and your community.
summary: Lambda Sigma Honorary

name: Korean Student Association
description:
The Korean Student Association has the following objectives:

  1. foster the education and enrichment of its members and the university community with aspects of the Korean culture
  2. support Korean community within campus
  3. connect Korean students to the Tuscaloosa Korean community
  4. maintain KSA representation in the University
summary: The Korean Student Association (KSA) is an organization that supports UA Korean students on campus and the Korean community in Tuscaloosa. KSA is also an organization for students interested in Korean culture.

name: The Druid Collective
description:
The Druid Collective is a co-ed, self-governing residential honor society. We are the oldest continuous honors program in existence at UA and elect our own officers and resident advisers. We provide on campus residential and social opportunities to make and maintain social bonds, intellectual discussion, and positive change on campus. 

 

In essence we are a counterculture fraternity.

 

Anyone interested can learn more at https://thedruidcollective.org/

Minimum academics are typically a 28 ACT, 1300 SAT or a 3.3 college GPA, however these are not hard requirements. After applying, applicants will be called in for an interview by a committee of active members who will interview each applicant before voting them in as active members or denying them. Membership fees are $75 / Semester ($40 for new members). Active members are expected to attend 2/3 of collective meetings, vote on collective choices, and generally help out.

 

The Druid Collective was formerly known as The Mallet Assembly
summary: The Druid Collective is a student-lead honors organization for academically-minded and independent young people. We offer opportunities for students to become further engaged in their education and community.

name: Marr's Field Journal
description: Marr's Field Journal, the University of Alabama's literary and arts magazine, was created in 1989 as a forum for emerging writers and artists. Marr's Field Journal strives to enrich its readers by showcasing exceptional undergraduate poetry, prose, art, and music compositions. The insights of the contributing writers and artists have made Marr's Field Journal an esteemed member of the Office of Student Media and a vital part of the creative community on campus. Marr's Field Journal is published online annually and offers an exhibition of student creative work.
summary: The Marr's Field Journal is UA's premier undergraduate art and literary journal. Its annual online publication showcases exceptional undergraduate poetry, prose, dance, theater, art, and music composition. Visit mfj.ua.edu to look at or submit your work.

name: MBA Association
description: We are the MBA Association at The University of Alabama. Our organization serves to create community outside of the classroom and bring student voices to the table on behalf of the MBA Candidates enrolled at The University of Alabama, Manderson Graduate School of Business. Through social and civic engagement, we seek to create an operating network of students, staff and alumni to encourage fellowship beyond the academic halls.
summary: MBA Association

name: Hillel Foundation
description: Hillel has been a welcoming home for Jewish students, their friends and those interested in meeting new people. We strive as an organization to provide social, cultural, and religious activities so Jewish students have a place to connect and have fun. Our students come from all over and are leaders in a wide variety of organizations across campus. We welcome new members to Hillel throughout the year and are delighted that our “family” continues to grow.
summary: Jewish Student Center

name: Honors College Ambassadors
description: The Honors College Ambassadors are a group of Honors Students that serve as the face of the Honors College for recruiting, special speakers, and other such events.
summary: The Honors College Ambassadors are a group of Honors Students that serve as the face of the Honors College for recruiting, special speakers, and other such events.

name: Honors College Assembly
description: The Honors College Assembly serves as representation for UA Honors College students. The tiered leadership structure of elected officers, directors, and committee members allows students to participate in the creation of events for Honors College students with a variety of passions. It is our goal to promote a community of multidimensional scholars and passionate individuals across the entire UA campus, and create a place where Honors students are proud to call home. In the past, our community building opportunities have included Honors College tailgates on gameday, seating in the Honors block in Bryant Denny Stadium, resume workshops, research fairs, and service days, among others.
summary: The Honors College Assembly serves as a representative body for all Honors College students by providing opportunities outside of the classroom for personal growth and community engagement.

name: Indian Students Association of Tuscaloosa
description:
The Indian Students Association of Tuscaloosa (ISAT) is a cultural organization at the University of Alabama that aims to exhibit Indian culture, traditions, and diversity with the UA diaspora. ISAT events celebrate India and its heritage with cultural music, dances, and delicious Indian food. The events are always free and open to all students, faculty/staff, and the community. In addition to its premier festive celebrations of Diwali – the festival of lights, and Holi - the festival of colors, the ISAT celebrates Indian Independence Day and International Yoga Day at UA, among other events.

 

ISAT aims to engage with the Indian community and build a close connection. It helps incoming Indian students by easing the transition process to the States and giving them a sense of a home away from home.

 

The organization’s efforts have been recognized and applauded at the Exemplo Capstone Awards with the Cultural Event of the year in 2024, Outstanding Cultural Organization of the Year in 2023, the Award for Collaborative Programming in 2022, and the Cultural Event of the Year for Diwali in 2021. We’re excited about the upcoming year and have planned a multitude of activities and events in the coming months. We look forward to seeing you at our events.
summary: Indian culture, festivals, and heritage; collaborative cultural and student-oriented events; Indian food; Cultural dance performances; Indian music; Diwali - the festival of lights; Holi -  the festival of colors; Indian movie nights & lots more!

name: Institute of Electrical and Electronic Engineers
description: IEEE is the world’s largest technical professional organization dedicated to advancing technology for the benefit of humanity. The UA IEEE student branch is a technical student community designed to help students' professional development. We meet biweekly, often inviting speakers and recruiters from within the industry to educate our members about the many career paths an electrical engineer can follow.
summary: The IEEE is the world’s largest technical professional organization dedicated to advancing technology for the benefit of humanity. The UA IEEE student branch is a technical student community designed to help students' professional development.

name: Interior Design Student Association
description: Interior Design Student Association is an umbrella organization combining ASID (American Society of Interior Designers), IIDA (International Interior Design Association), and NKBA (National Kitchen and Bath Association).
summary: Interior Design, ASID, IIDA, NKBA

name: International Students Association
description:
The purposes and aims of ISA are:

 

1. To give international students a voice on campus, assist their transition to UA, and enhance their UA experience;

 

2. To increase interaction between students from different countries and cultures and encourage international students' involvement on campus; and

 

3. To generate interest in and spread awareness about the cultural diversity at UA and facilitate relationship building and communication across cultures.
summary: The association enhances international students' UA experience by promoting interaction between different nationalities and cultures.

name: Kappa Alpha Order - Alpha Beta
description: Kappa Alpha is a distinguished social fraternity on campus with a variety of young men from across the Southeast. We participate in academic, athletic, and philanthropic events throughout the year. We seek to create a life-long experience of brotherhood and excellence for our members, which they can take with them into the next chapters of their lives.
summary: Fraternity focused on developing young men through philanthropic activities, social initiatives, intramural recreation, professional career training, and brotherhood events.

name: Kappa Alpha Psi
description: On March 17, 1974, the dream of our illustrious founders made its presence on the campus of the predominately White University of Alabama. Eta Chi became the first Black Greek-lettered organization at the Capstone. Starting as an extension of the Epsilon Epsilon chapter at nearby Stillman College, we received our charter and name on February 8, 1975. The chapter strives to meet the expectations of the fraternity's fundamental purpose of achievement. The first Black SGA president at The University of Alabama was Cleophus Thomas, a member of Eta Chi. Being a member of the Southern Province, we are one of the few chapters in the province to have two Guy L. Grant award winners, Cleophus Thomas and Keflyn Reed. Spring 2001 inductee Robert Turner is the very first student to hold the position of executive chief of staff; for the first time in UA SGA history, two chiefs of staff were selected. Such outstanding achievements are why we are the "Glowing Diamond" of Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity, Inc. With the emergence of the Eta Chi chapter, our founder's dream has become a reality.
summary: On March 17, 1974, the dream of our illustrious founders made its presence on the campus of the predominately White University of Alabama. Eta Chi became the first black, Greek-lettered organization at the Capstone.

name: Kappa Alpha Theta
summary: Kappa Alpha Theta was the first Greek Letter Fraternity known among women. Our chapter has 400+ members who are involved in over 60 different organizations on campus and dedicate time to our national philanthropy C.A.S.A and The B+ Foundation.

name: Kappa Delta
summary: The Zeta Chapter of Kappa Delta at the University of Alabama was the first women’s sorority on the UA campus & the oldest chapter to have been active continuously since its chartering. Our chapter has a strong sisterhood & commitment to community service

name: Men's Lacrosse Club
description: Men's Lacrosse Club of the University of Alabama playing in MCLA Divison I in the South Eastern Lacrosse Conference.
summary: MCLA Division 1 Men's Lacrosse team

name: Men's Club Soccer
description:
2023 Region II Mountain West Division Champions

2024 Region II Mountain West Division Champions

Visit our website at https://alabamamenssoccer.com/

--Fall 2025 INFO--

Welcome to Men's Club Soccer at the University of Alabama!

Follow us on Insta @uamenssoccer for further announcements regarding schedule and team updates. Contact President Austin Ayo or VP Emerson Maughan for any immediate questions.

________________________

The Men's Soccer Club is one of UA's oldest sport clubs. Tryouts occur at the beginning of every Fall Semester with a $20 tryout fee. If selected for the team, membership dues pay for hotel rooms, travel costs, uniform costs, tournament entry fees, and refereeing fees. Players may tryout throughout the season by invitation only.
summary: Alabama Men's Soccer, UA Men's Soccer, Men's Club Soccer, Alabama Men's Club Soccer

name: Men's Wheelchair Basketball
description: UA Men's Intercollegiate Wheelchair Basketball team.
summary: men's, wheelchair, basketball

name: Minority Association of Pre-Medical Students
description: The Minority Association of Pre-Medical Students represents students from historically underrepresented backgrounds of the Student National Medical Association pursuing careers in healthcare (Medicine, Dentistry, Physical Therapy, Nursing, Optometry, etc). The Student National Medical Association (SNMA) is committed to supporting current and future underrepresented minority medical students, addressing the needs of underserved communities, and increasing the number of clinically excellent, culturally competent, and socially conscious healthcare providers.
summary: The Minority Association of Pre-Medical Students represents students from historically underrepresented backgrounds of the Student National Medical Association pursuing careers in healthcare (Medicine, Dentistry, Physical Therapy, Nursing, Optometry, etc

name: Mortar Board
description: Established in 1918 to recognize oustanding senior college women, Mortar Board continues to recognize now men and women for their leadership, scholarship and service.
summary: Achievement in Scholarship, Leadership, Service

name: The National Council of Negro Women
description: The National Council of Negro Women was founded by Mary McLeod Bethune in 1935. Bethune, born to former slaves a decade after the end of the Civil War, believed that through education African Americans could begin to prosper in a country that still opposed racial equality. She devoted her life to ensuring that African Americans had the right to education as well as to freedom from discrimination. More specifically, Bethune recognized the necessity to extend the leadership of black women through a national organization.In 1935, Bethune called together 28 national women leaders to form "an organization of organizations," a council that became the National Council of Negro Women, Inc. (NCNW). Bethune envisioned NCNW serving as a clearinghouse, facilitating networking and coalition building, and advocating the use of collective power on issues affecting women, their families, and their communities. Today, NCNW sponsors educational, economic, social, and cultural projects nationally and locally to achieve equality of opportunity. Additionally, NCNW strives to eliminate prejudice and discrimination based upon race, creed, color, sex, or national origin.The University of Alabama Section of NCNW was founded in January 1998 by a group of young, independent, African American women that had a vision of empowering the female African American student population through NCNW.The mission of the National Council of Negro Women, Inc. is to advance opportunities and improve the quality of life for African American women, their familes, and communities. NCNW fulfills this mission through research, advocacy, and national and community-based services and programs in the United States and African countries.
summary: Women, Education, Minority, Community Service, Cultural, Sisterhood

name: National Pan-Hellenic Council
description: On May 10, 1930, on the campus of Howard University, in Washington DC, the National Pan-Hellenic Council was formed as a permanent organization with the following charter members: Omega Psi Phi and Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternities, and Alpha Kappa Alpha, Delta Sigma Theta and Zeta Phi Beta Sororities. In 1931, Alpha Phi Alpha and Phi Beta Sigma Fraternities joined the Council. Sigma Gamma Rho Sorority joined in 1937 and Iota Phi Theta Fraternity completed the list of member organizations in 1997.
summary: The National Pan-Hellenic Council at The University of Alabama is the governing body of eight historically African-American Greek Letter Organizations.

name: National Society of Collegiate Scholars
description: The National Society of Collegiate Scholars is a national honors organization recognized by the Association of College Honor Societies that distinguishes high achieving first and second-year college students, while providing them an opportunity to gain leadership experience and give back to their local communities. NSCS not only recognizes academic excellence, but cultivated leadership, teamwork, volunteering, and experience to prepare its members for future career success.The criteria for membership is first and second-year students who rank in the top 20 percent of their class and have a minimum GP of 3.4.
summary: Scholarship, leadership, service, integrity, honor, society, organization, community

name: National Student Speech Language and Hearing Association
description: UA National Student Speech Language Hearing Association (NSSLHA) - a local chapter of a national organization for graduate and undergraduate students interested in studying communicative disorders, speech-language pathology, and audiology.
summary: The University of Alabama's National Student Speech Language Hearing Association (NSSLHA) is an organization for both undergraduate and graduate pre-professional Speech-Language Pathologists and Audiologists.

name: The Navigators
description: Would you like your life to count for Jesus Christ? Want a greater vision to influence your university and your friends for Him? Interested in a community of friends who love and inspire you to great things for God? Here you can get involved in a dynamic Bible study on campus to dig deep into God's Word and live it out for His glory. Come and experience the power of discipleship and a deeper walk with the Lord. The Navigators college ministry is no ordinary ministry! Our aim is to change the world for Christ one life at a time at your campus, across the country, and into the nations. Our passions are Jesus and college students. We strive to know Christ and to make Him known, and we invite you to join us in that pursuit!
summary: christian ministry, Navigators, Navs, Bama Navs, discipleship, mentoring, Bible study, small group

name: National Society of Black Engineers
description:
The mission of The National Society of Black Engineers is "to increase the number of culturally responsible Black Engineers who excel academically, succeed professionally, and positively impact the community."

We strive to create a family on campus at the University of Alabama. UA NSBE hosts events that focus on professional development, academic development, and community service. We aim to highlight the importance of a good résumé and cover letter, the power of networking, whether in-person or on social media. We promote professional communication with professors, engaging with peers, and the utilization of resources. We give structure on studying, focusing, with heavy emphasis on time management, and the balance of it all; there is so much more. Moreover, we also make sure that we are all alright and have plenty of times to enjoy ourselves together. Real change can be made when we work together and that's what this organization is all about. If you are looking for a community to connect with that wants the best for you and will tell you the truth at all times, join this organization because everything is for you.
summary: NSBE is a professional engineering organization with strong focus in nurturing and developing underrepresented engineering students. We accomplish this by hosting events that focus on professional development, academic development, and community service.

name: New College Council
description: The New College Council and Executive Council work to build a strong New College community through coordinated events and outreach. This organization works in the best interest of inclusiveness and support for all New College students and faculty members and aids in providing student opportunities by promoting and maintaining the academic integrity of New College.
summary: The New College Council and Executive Council work to build a strong New College community through coordinated events and outreach. This organization works in the best interest of inclusion and support for all New College students and faculty members.

name: The Dance Honor Society of Alabama
description: Founded in 2017, The Dance Honor Society of Alabama is The University of Alabama's first on-campus honor society centered around dance. DHSA is founded on four pillars derived from the name of the organization: passion for performance (Dance), academic excellence (Honor), service (Society), and creating community (Alabama). We fulfill these four commitments by participating in UA Theatre & Dance performances, providing academic support to members, donating to and partnering with organizations in our community, and hosting social events for members.
summary: A dance-centered honor society dedicated to performance, academic excellence, service, and community.

name: Omega Psi Phi Fraternity, Inc.
description:
Omega Psi Phi Fraternity, Inc. was founded on Friday evening, November 17, 1911, on the campus of Howard University by three undergraduate students with the guidance of their faculty advisor. The fraternity adopted the cardinal principles of Manhood, Scholarship, Perseverance, and Uplift, along with the motto, “Friendship is Essential to the Soul.” 

The Beta Eta Chapter was established at The University of Alabama on April 20, 1974. Now celebrating 51 years of excellence, we remain committed to upholding the traditions and values laid by our founders and those who came before us. As we reflect on our legacy, we also look forward to continuing the work of impact, brotherhood, and uplift on our campus and beyond.
summary: Omega Psi Phi Fraternity, Inc., the first fraternity founded on an HBCU campus, was established at Howard University in 1911. Beta Eta Chapter was chartered at The University of Alabama in 1974 and proudly celebrates 51 years of brotherhood and uplift.

name: Omicron Delta Kappa - Iota Circle
description:
Omicron Delta Kappa, the National Leadership Honor Society, recognizes and encourages superior leadership and exemplary character. O∆K encourages collaboration among members across the five pillars of leadership celebrated by O∆K: academics and research, athletics, service to campus and community, communications, and creative and performing arts. Founded in 1914 at Washington and Lee University, O∆K honors and develops leaders through scholarships, workshops, career development, leadership resources, and a lifelong connection to other members. The Society also champions its leadership values of collaboration, inclusivity, integrity, scholarship, and service.  

With more than 300 circles of record at colleges and universities across the United States, ODK is committed to developing campus leaders who will become tomorrow’s community leaders. O∆K also encourages collaboration among leaders across campus and believes that strong leaders become stronger leaders through this fellowship. In addition to undergraduate students—membership is open to faculty, staff, alumni, and graduate students because ODK believes that a diverse cross-section of the campus community strengthens each circle and allows students to learn from people in different stages of life. Membership in ODK is a mark of the highest distinction and honor.
summary: Omicron Delta Kappa, the National Leadership Honor Society, recognizes and encourages superior leadership and exemplary character. OΔK fosters collaboration among members across five phases: scholarship, athletics, service, communications, and arts.

name: Order of Omega
description:
The Order of Omega is a leadership honor society for college fraternity and sorority members that recognizers individuals with high academic achievement and service. It is an honor society for established Greek leaders, typically requiring a 3.0+ GPA and junior/senior status, selecting the top 3-5% of the campus Greek population. 

https://orderofomega.org/
summary: national honorary for fraternity and sorority members

name: The University of Alabama Undergraduate Historical Society
description: The University of Alabama Undergraduate Historical Society brings together history majors and minors, as well as history professors. We host professionalization events that will help history majors prepare for their careers. Each year we organize different events to help history majors both develop themselves professionally and bring historically minded undergraduates together. We host historical events from professor panels, trivia nights, movie screenings, and more. We are affiliated with Phi Alpha Theta which is an American honor society for undergraduate and graduate students and professors of history.
summary: History, Honor Society, Undergraduate

name: Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity, Inc. (Theta Delta Chapter)
description: The vision of the founders of Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity came to the campus of the University of Alabama on March 23, 1976. The vision was lead by the Honorable Bro. Ed Walton, the founder of the Theta Delta Chapter.During the Fall Semester of 1975, the Beta Pi Sigma Graduate Chapter of Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity began consideration to establish a new chapter at the University of Alabama. Beginning on September 12, 1975, a series of interest meetings were held throughout the fall, on November 16, 1975 the selections of four candidate for membership into Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity was made. The four aspirants were Carl Cook, Kambeya wa Kambeya, Alfred Page, and Benjamin Thomas, Jr. Later two addition members were added, Billy Henderson and Jeffery Holsey, to surpass the requirement of five charter members.On December 3, 1975, following a meeting with the potential members, Brother Walton, on behalf of the six men, submitted a formal petition to the University's Office of Student Life and the Interfraternity Council for a charter to be a recognized Greek-letter student organization. In addition to the names of the six men, the petition included three members of Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity who were in graduate school at the University of Alabama: Brother Charles McPherson, Brother Larry Ruby, and Brother Lonzell Wilson, whose inclusion enabled the Interfraternity Council to recognize the petition as meeting its required numbers.On December 5, 1975, a letter from the Office of the National Headquarters Building in Washington, DC, reached the Dean of Student Life at the University of Alabama, to notify the University of its approval of the establishment of a new chapter at the University. The new chapter came into official existence in January 1976, as five of the six candidates were initiated in the Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity. Benjamin Thomas was the lone exception due to his transfer to the University of Alabama at Birmingham following the Fall Semester of 1975. The Charter for the Chapter became official on March 23, 1976, including the names of the five Charter Members, and the chapter was anointed as the THETA DELTA CHAPTER.Since that time, Theta Delta has been a beacon of light to the community, and produced some of the most influential men around the University and the world.
summary: Phi Beta Sigma, The Fraternity of Inclusiveness, founded on January 9, 1914 on the Campus of Howard University in Washington, DC.

name: Phi Delta Theta
description:
Phi Delta Theta
Alabama Alpha
145+ Years of Excellence at the Capstone
 
The Alabama Alpha chapter of Phi Delta Theta was originally the sixth fraternity at the University of Alabama and has a long and storied history at the University (both in and out of the classroom). "The Phi House" received its charter from the national fraternity on October 17, 1877 making it the first Phi Chapter in the state of Alabama, thus deemed the "Alpha" chapter. 
 
The Founding members of Alabama Alpha are as follows:
  • 1 Oliver F. Smith
  • 2 Thomas M. Hobbs
  • 3 Thomas H. Spencer
  • 4 John J. Mickle
  • 5 Matthew R. Knox
  • 6 Ernest McCreary
  • 7 James C. Smith
  • 8 John S. Mooring  
 
A University ban on all fraternities caused the chapter to run SUB ROSA until 1883. Meetings were then allowed and were initially held in Woods Hall and later in Philomatic Hall. Members began a search for a suitable house in 1905 and within 3 years, a chapter house was built on the Pelham Brown lot on University Ave. The chapter house was relocated to 911 University Blvd in 1920 where it stood until the fire of 1957. The Phi House was then rebuilt in the same location and remained on University Blvd just steps away from Bryant Denny Stadium until 2012.  
 
The newly constructed chapter house was completed in time for the active members to move in for the fall '12 semester. Modeled after Mount Vernon, the new Phi Delta Theta house is still located on University Blvd.  The Phi House upgraded from a beloved, but very dilapidated facility, to arguably the nicest house on campus. 
 
For 145+ years, the Alabama Alpha chapter of Phi Delta Theta has been one the most outstanding chapters at the University of Alabama and plans to continue this great tradition for many years to come.
 
 
Alabama Alpha and The Crimson Tide
 
University of Alabama law student William G. Little learned how to play American football while attending prep school in Andover, MA and began teaching the sport to fellow Alabama students in early 1892. Later in the year, the school formed an official team of 19 players, with Little as captain and E. B. Beaumont as head coach. Among those on the team was William B. Bankhead, future U.S. Speaker of the House and brother in the Alabama Alpha Chapter of Phi Delta Theta. At the time, the team was referred to as the "Cadets", the "Crimson White", or simply the "Varsity."
 
John Forney was a brother in the Alabama Alpha Chapter of Phi Delta Theta and is best known as the voice of the Alabama Crimson Tide for many years.
summary: For 145+ years, the Alabama Alpha Chapter of Phi Delta Theta has been one of the most outstanding fraternities at the University of Alabama.

name: Phi Sigma Pi National Honor Fraternity
description:
Thank you for your interest in the Delta Beta Chapter of Phi Sigma Pi National Honor Fraternity!

Phi Sigma Pi National Honor Fraternity is a gender-inclusive fraternity open to undergraduate students attending four-year colleges and universities throughout the United States. Our purpose is to bring together some of the brightest and most motivated students on campus and work together in a spirit of excellence to encourage three ideals:

  • The acquisition and dissemination of information and knowledge through scholarship.
  • The application of professional skills and the fostering of leadership qualities by promoting and advancing the welfare of humanity.
  • The fostering of non-discriminatory, fraternal fellowship within our ranks.

Collectively, these three ideals are known as "The Tripod" of Phi Sigma Pi. Joining Phi Sigma Pi is the first step in a lifelong journey of increased awareness, improved capabilities, greater opportunities and a network of friendships. Phi Sigma Pi is open for membership to both male and female students who possess the following credentials:

  • You have completed a minimum of 12 hours of college work.
  • You have achieved a minimum 3.25 cumulative GPA on a 4.00 scale.
  • You have at least two semesters left in your undergraduate career

Our members tend to be some of the most involved students on campus. We routinely have members who are also involved in social fraternities or sororities, honor societies, university athletics, student government, and many other organizations. We are not part of the social Greek system. We support involvement in other organizations and membership in Phi Sigma Pi poses no conflict with membership in any other campus organization.
summary: We are an all majors, gender-inclusive honor fraternity. Our brotherhood revolves around three attributes: scholarship, leadership and fellowship. There are brothers from every part of campus. We proudly represent the Capstone.

name: Project Health
description:
Our mission as Project Health is to serve as the go-to source of health education information on the University of Alabama campus by actively engaging ourselves and the campus community in health and wellness programming.

Project Health's membership is comprised of Health Advocates, Health Ambassadors, Health Hut and Gamma Interns.

Health Hut: Health Hut Interns work two 2-hour shifts each week in the Hut! Interns engage visitors with games, activities, and conversations aimed at increasing student awareness about health issues and behaviors that directly affect them, increase student knowledge about healthy behaviors and making healthy choices, and reduce the barriers students perceive to improving their health. The Hut is on campus for 36 hours each week, Monday through Thursday. The Department of Health Promotion & Wellness of the Student Health Center supports this program and provides oversight of the health content in outreach and programming materials.

Health Advocates: The Health Advocates play an essential role in providing the social support needed for individuals to begin and continue making healthy choices. The Health Advocates provide monthly awareness events all over campus on various health topics, and also cover weekly national awareness campaigns. Advocates participate in weekly “informal interactions” in which they have casual conversations with other students on campus about a health topic.
The Health Advocates are also responsible for creating health advocacy events. Each Health Advocate team is creates one awareness event per semester. Follow the Health Advocates on social media to stay up to date on when the events are being hosted!

Health Ambassadors: The Health Ambassadors are the primary movers on programming in residence halls, classes, and student organizations and our Healthy Hump Day, an interactive tabling event every Wednesday about national awareness weeks and months. Health Ambassadors work on health topic committees to help develop and implement programs as well as develop materials for outreach. Ambassadors work approximately 5 hours per week, and sometimes more, working bi-weekly at healthy hump day and doing programming.

Gamma: Gamma peer education interns provide health information and targeted programming aimed at increasing health knowledge and promoting healthy decision making to members of Greek Organizations.
The goal of GAMMA is to provide members for Greek organizations with information and support to encourage healthy lifestyle choices in chapters. This is achieved through weekly “Health Minute” presentations at chapter meetings and tailored educational programs planned and implemented by GAMMA delegates and HPW Health Educators. This is in efforts to engage members of Greek organizations in increasing their awareness about a wide variety of health topics in addition to increasing their knowledge about health topics most pertinent to their chapter.

All members in Project Health must apply and be interviewed prior to being selected.  It is a very competitive program and requires a minimum of 4 hours per week commitment from all of our students.

If you want to be a leader, help educate your peers on health, and serve the UA Community - this organization is a great place to start!
summary: Peer Health Education through our interns within 5 divisions - Health Hut, Health Ambassadors, Health Advocates, Gamma, and Healthy Minds

name: Sigma Gamma Epsilon Alpha Chi Chapter
description:
Purpose and Objective

The Society of Sigma Gamma Epsilon was established to recognize scholarship and professionalism in the Earth Sciences. It has for its objectives the scholastic, scientific, and professional advancement of its members and the extension of relations of friendship and assistance among colleges and universities which are devoted to the advancement of the Earth Sciences.

History and Government

The Society was founded on March 30, 1915, at The University of Kansas. Nearly 200 chapters throughout the United States have been installed since 1915. Government of the Society is by student members and the ultimate legislative authority is vested in a National Convention held every two years. It is composed of one student delegate from each chapter and the seven national officers who are faculty members.  The Constitution and Bylaws of the Society are located here.

How Can I Join?

Any person in any branch of the Earth Sciences who has completed at least 10 semester hours or 15 quarter hours in Earth Science courses and has maintained a minimum 3.0 G.P.A. (on a 4.0 system) in all Earth Science courses together with an overall G.P.A. of 2.67 in all college courses is qualified for membership. If you qualify and wish to join, you need only inform any officer or the Advisor of your department’s chapter and they can propose you for membership. Initiation of new members is typically held both fall and spring semesters.

Why Should I Join?

Membership in Sigma Gamma Epsilon and the listing of it on one’s résumé (or the wearing of a member's pin) tells a prospective employer or a colleague that you are at least a "B" average student and that in the eyes of your peers you are professionally motivated. Members serve their departments in a number of ways such as organizing field trips, tutoring, arranging displays, etc. Many chapters have money-raising activities to obtain funds to use for scholarships and awards or to purchase items needed for student use or to assist the department in other ways.  Members of Sigma Gamma Epsilon are encouraged to submit articles to the Society’s professional journal, The Compass, as it provides the opportunity to share your research with the Society. The Compass contains student papers and articles by practicing earth scientists in addition to news and notes about the Society. As an associated society of the Geological Society of America, Sigma Gamma Epsilon members are entitled to GSA-member rates for registration at national and regional meetings and for purchase of GSA publications.

Fees and Dues

A one-time initiation fee of $25.00 is charged. Once you are initiated you are always a member of the Society; however, while you are a student you pay annual National Dues of $15.00, plus any local chapter dues. After leaving school, you may become an Active Alumni Member by a payment of $15.00 per year to the National Office, or a single fee of $175.00 for a lifetime membership.
summary: Alpha Chi chapter of Sigma Gamma Epsilon is a national honor society for the Earth sciences.

description: The Tau Beta Pi Association was founded at Lehigh University in 1885 by Edward Higginson Williams, Jr., to mark in a fitting manner those who have conferred honor upon their Alma Mater by distinguished scholarship and exemplary character as students in engineering, or by their attainments as alumni in the field of engineering, and to foster a spirit of liberal culture in engineering colleges.The Alabama Beta Chapter of Tau Beta was founded at the University of Alabama on November 20, 1926. name: Tau Sigma Transfer Student National Honor Society
description:
Tau Sigma is an honor society for transfer students that have a 3.5 GPA their first semester after transferring from another four year university, junior college, or community college. They must have attended the previous institution for at least one year. For more information, visit the Tau Sigma national website.

Invitations for new membership are sent in the spring of each academic year and Induction is held in April.

name: Theta Tau
description:
The purpose of Theta Tau is to develop and maintain a high standard of professional interest among its members and to unite them in a strong bond of fraternal fellowship. 

We believe that brotherhood and fraternal fellowship are an invaluable part of an engineering education. With this in mind, we participate in a wide variety of activities and host events designed to increase the strength of our brotherhood, give back to our campus and the Tuscaloosa community, and ensure our brothers’ future success in engineering, no matter what discipline of engineering that may be. Since our chapter’s founding in 1922, we have initiated over 2000 members. 

We are always searching for new brothers! Any students in the College of Engineering can email rush@muthetatau.org and we can get you in touch with us! Also, check us out on Facebook and Instagram!
summary: We are the co-ed professional and social fraternity for engineers at the University of Alabama. Our membership is open to all students enrolled in the College of Engineering.

name: Tide 4 Christ
description:
It is our goal to embody the love of Christ and to show this love to every single student on this campus. We strive to do this by serving others and denying ourselves (Philippians 2). The mercy and grace of God toward the Church is why we exist, but this gift is open for all. Tide 4 Christ has regular meeting times each week: Sunday morning at 9:00am for Bible class with worship at 10:00am, Monday night at 7:00pm for a student-led devotional, and Wednesday night at 6:00 pm for a free meal and Bible class. These events take place at Central Church of Christ (304 Hargrove Road). We also offer LIFE groups every Sunday night where we meet in each other’s homes to study God’s Word. We would absolutely love to meet you and for you to be a part of the Church so please reach out if you have any questions. Make sure to follow us on Instagram and Twitter (@tide4christ) for updates on events!

You can find out more about the churches of Christ by following this link.
summary: Tide 4 Christ's mission is to love like Christ loves. We are not perfect people but God's grace covers our imperfections. To us, Christ is more than a Sunday morning service. He is an identity. Tide 4 Christ is affiliated with Central Church of Christ.

name: Turkish Student Association
description:
The Turkish Student Association is a non-profit organization dedicated to addressing the social and cultural needs of Turkish and American students at The University of Alabama. Our mission is to promote mutual understanding between American and Turkish cultures and to foster community through cultural and educational activities.

We strive to help students become better integrated into their local communities and to contribute to peace, prosperity, and harmony in our area. We believe that getting to know one another is the first step toward building bridges between our communities.

Membership Statement:
"Membership in registered student organizations shall be open to all students of The University of Alabama, without regard to race, religion, sex, ability status, national origin, color, age, gender identity, gender expression, sexual identity, or veteran status—except in cases of designated fraternal organizations exempted by federal law from Title IX regulations concerning discrimination on the basis of sex."
summary: Turkish Students, American Students, Turkey,  The University of Alabama, Tuscaloosa, Alabama, Turks, Turk, American, Turk ogrenci, Turkish

description: The University of Alabama Bowling Club provides students the opportunity to practice and participate in intercollegiate bowling and compete at the highest level
summary: The UA Bowling Club travels across the country to compete in tournaments in USBC Collegiate competition. Our bowlers compete at the highest level against other top programs in the country.

name: UA College Democrats
description:
The University of Alabama College Democrats is the largest political organization at the Capstone. UA Dems is officially chartered through College Democrats of America and the Alabama College Democrats State Federation. 

Throughout the year, we organize projects and events around campus, in Tuscaloosa, and throughout the state of Alabama.

  • Provide education material to students to learn more about politics and their role in politics
  • Register students to vote throughout the year
  • Work on grassroots campaigns in Tuscaloosa, in Alabama, and on national elections
  • Coordinate with politicians and government employees to host high profile speakers
  • Travel to political conferences to learn how to manage and operate political campaigns
  • Host weekly social gatherings, dinners, movie nights, bowling, and other events


If you have any questions, please email uadems@gmail.com. Our weekly newsletter will be sent from this email. If you have signed up and are not receiving it, make sure to check your spam. If it is there, then mark the email as not junk.
summary: Officially chartered and recognized through the Alabama College Democrats State Federation, College Democrats of America, Alabama Democratic Party, and the Democratic National Committee.

name: The University of Alabama College Republicans
description:
Our mission is to inform the student body of the principles of the Republican Party on the campus of the University of Alabama. We also strive to help our members gain skills to better articulate those principles and advance their values on and off campus. We seek to provide opportunities for members to work within the party and government at the local, state, and national levels.

 

Follow us on social media!

Facebook | Instagram
summary: The UACRs is committed to advancing the values of the Republican Party at UA. We provide a place for like-minded UA students to connect, meet elected officials and candidates, and discuss current political issues.

The club focuses on providing resources to its members on educational, professional, and community development. We focus on promoting health and wellness to benefit the University of Alabama and our community. We help our members network with staff and other kinesiology students, gain volunteer hours, and become active in research. If you are interested in participating or have any questions, please feel free to reach out to our primary contact, Sara Heard!

name: UA Men's Club Volleyball
description: We are the Alabama Men's Volleyball Club team. With both an A & B team, we practice 3 times per week and compete in 3-4 tournaments per semester, with our season concluding with the NCVF Club Volleyball National Championships.  We compete in the highest division of the NCVF (D1A) and have been ranked top 25 in the country the past 3 seasons. While some of our tournaments are at home and in the south, we also travel to the Midwest and east coast for other highly competitive tournaments, one of which is the Hoosier-Illini classic, where, this last season, we beat multiple top 10 teams. Tryouts are held each semester; please reach out to the current president, listed below, for any further questions.
summary: volleyball, mens, athletics

name: The University of Alabama's Chapter of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
description:
The vision of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People is to ensure a society in which all individuals have equal rights without discrimination based on race.

  • To ensure the political, educational, social, and economic equality of all citizens
  • To achieve equality of rights and eliminate race prejudice among the citizens of the United States
  • To remove all barriers of racial discrimination through democratic processes
  • To seek enactment and enforcement of federal, state, and local laws securing civil rights
  • To inform the public of the adverse effects of racial discrimination and to seek its elimination
  • To educate persons as to their constitutional rights and to take all lawful action to secure the exercise thereof, and to take any other lawful action in furtherance of these objectives, consistent with the NAACP’s Articles of Incorporation and this Constitution.
summary: The mission of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People is to ensure the political, educational, social, and economic equality of rights of all persons and to eliminate race-based discrimination.

name: UA Rugby Football Club
description: The University of Alabama Rugby Football Club is a fellowship of athletes dedicated to the playing and promotion of Rugby Union Football at The University of Alabama.  The club’s ideal is to engage in vigorous athletic competition in a manner that will bring pride to individual members and bind them together as teammates and friends, and that will be a source of pride and honor to the club as a whole, to The University of Alabama, and the sport of Rugby Union Football.
summary: rugby, UARFC, sports club, athletics, bama rugby, Alabama Rugby

name: UA Sailing Club
description: The UA Sailing Club is designed for University of Alabama students to gain experience sailing and racing. We are a part of ICSA and race in regattas at other universities. We practice at the Tuscaloosa Sailing Club, located on Lake Tuscaloosa. Our club has access to multiple boats ranging in size from 12-foot Sunfish to 18-foot Buccaneers and Flying Scots (and soon 420s). We hold practice in both the fall and spring. In the fall we typically practice on away-game Sundays as to not interfere with football games (Roll Tide!). To join the club you do not need any previous sailing experience, just be able to pass a swim test. Dues for the club are paid for with either daily passes or a three outing bundle. The sailing club has a clubhouse, campground, showers, bathroom and full kitchen that we utilize for outings. To find out about current events please check out our Instagram: @uasailing. To join please request to join on the SOURCE and join our GroupMe link (in our Instagram bio!) For any additional information or questions, please email us at uasailing@gmail.com
summary: The purpose of the University of Alabama Sailing Club is to engage UA students in the lifelong skill of sailing. No experience is needed to join, just a positive attitude and a drive to learn. Come have fun sailing Lake Tuscaloosa with us!

name: The Sustained Dialogue Club
description: Sustained Dialogue emerged at the University of Alabama due to the recognized need for meaningful conversations on our campus. Through Sustained Dialogue, we strive to positively impact our campus through the facilitation of peaceful, constructive dialogues that address issues pertinent to our campus and community. Because of UA's turbulent history, we hope that SD's presence and action on our campus can signify the progress that has been made. SD began at Princeton University under the direction of two pioneering students, with guidance from Dr. Harold Saunders, Director of International Affairs at the Kettering Foundation and President of the International Institute for Sustained Dialogue. UA joined the Sustained Dialogue Campus Network in 2006, initiating a change process on campus that has been slow, but building. This year we wish to reach out to more students than ever before. Our organization has been reinvigorated by the astounding support of the Crossroads Civic Engagement Center, along with the Office of Community Affairs. With more interest than ever before, SD has a renewed sense of how students can impact one another through simple communication and mutual understanding.
summary: In addition to placing students in groups of 8-12 diverse participants who then meet once weekly for one semester or academic year, SD Club promotes dialogue-to-action with regard to social issues and identity across campus.

name: UA Vedic Society
description: The purposes of the UA Vedic Society are to, (1) help academic body and the society at large discern spirit from matter and the eternal from temporary, (2) encourage the student community to appreciate and understand higher dimensions of knowledge as given in the Vedic scriptures, (3) discuss modern issues in the light of the Vedic scriptures and encourage modern thinkers to appreciate and apply ancient spiritual ideas, (4) help youth become effective managers of their own lives and powerful leaders in the society, (5) encourage all youth to espouse eternal principles as given in the Vedic scriptures that would help them develop stable, well-rounded personalities, (6) provide opportunities for involvement and bring out one's inherent skills so they can do what they are made for in order to have fulfillment and satisfaction, and (7) provide proper understanding of different types of Yoga systems and encourage their practice.
summary: Spiritual, vegetarianism, Yoga, Meditation, Mantra, Soul, Spirit, Eternal

Alabama Men’s Club Water Polo is an intercollegiate water polo club at the University of Alabama. We compete in the CWPA Southeast Conference during the fall semester and participate in invitational tournaments around the country during the spring.

Southeast Division Champions: 2017, 2018, 2019, 2022, 2023, and 2025

Practices take place at the Aquatics Center from 7:00–9:00 p.m. during the fall and from 5:30–7:00 p.m. during the spring.

description: We are a club rugby team that is welcoming to everyone and anyone that is interested in playing, regardless of experience level. We practice Tuesday and Thursday from 7-9 p.m. on the lower recreation fields behind the fraternity houses. We are the only field with uprights. Also, we play year-round, so it's never too late to join!

name: UA Women's Ultimate Team
description: Ramma Jamma Ultimate was founded in 2009 by a very small group of UA women who shared a passion for Ultimate. Today, our team has grown to nearly 30 girls, who are determined to make a name for themselves in the competitive ultimate community. As a team, we practice and compete year-round with most of our tournaments in the spring semester. We travel throughout the southeast to compete against teams such as Auburn, LSU, Georgia, Vanderbilt, Florida State, Emory, Central Florida, and many more. While we are a very competitive team, we never forget the importance of having fun. We love hanging out together outside of practice, and our teammates are some of our closest friends. We are always looking to grow our team, and encourage anyone who is interested to come out and play!

name: United Greek Council
description:
The Alabama United Greek Council, commonly referred to as UGC, is the governing body for the nine multicultural and special interest based sororities and fraternities at The University of Alabama. Established on the UA campus in spring 2009, UGC exists to enable member organizations to share ideas and resources, promote mutual respect and equality, and provide a support network for students on campus.

The United Greek Council is comprised of ten special interest fraternities and sororities.

Alpha Omega Epsilon - an engineering sorority;

Alpha Kappa Psi - a co-educational professional fraternity;

Delta Sigma Pi - a co-educational business fraternity;

Kappa Alpha Pi - a co-educational pre-law and government fraternity;

Kappa Kappa Psi - a co-educational national honor band fraternity;

Phi Mu Alpha Sinfonia, a music fraternity;

Phi Sigma Pi, a national co-educational honor fraternity;

Theta Tau - a co-educational engineering fraternity;

Sigma Alpha Iota - a music sorority;

Phi Sigma Rho- a STEM sorority.
summary: The United Greek Council is the governing body for 8 special interest-based sororities and fraternities. The primary purpose of the UGC is to provide a support network for member organizations to share ideas and resources.

name: University Amateur Radio Club
description: The University Amateur Radio Club exists to facilitate the exchange of information and collegial relationships among members, to promote radio knowledge and technical competence, to increase individual radio operating skills and to conduct club activities that advance the general welfare and interest level of its members. UARC also serves the health and safety of the public by volunteering its expertise and support to the emergency communication needs of our community and region.
summary: Ham Radio, Amateur Radio, W4UAL, Electronics, Engineering, Broadcasting

name: Reach Campus Ministry
description:
We're Reach and we're here for you! We love college students. Our lives were changed by Jesus through campus ministry and we want you to experience the belonging, identity, and purpose that comes from loving God and making disciples.
 
We know college is an exciting but challenging time. College can be a really stressful time with studying for tests, paying for books, finding friends, awkward roommates, and still making enough time for naps.
 
That's why we're here! We want to be a family that lives life with you and together experience the transformative love of Jesus. 
 
Yep it's that simple. We're all about Jesus. We believe that 2,000 years ago Jesus died on a cross and rose from the grave to free us from sin and to secure a relationship with our Creator. We're staking our life on following Him into a selfless life for the good of others now and the hope of life with our Father forever. 
 
We hope you have a great year and if you need anything we're here for you! 
 
Campus Minister, Dylan Kirkland
summary: Making disciples of Jesus at the University of Alabama since 1954.

We host weekly meetings that feature guest speakers from companies around the country, as well as have a web dev team, a competitive programming team, study groups, and guaranteed free food!

name: The Wesley Foundation
description:
At Bama Wesley, we exist for one reason: to be a place where anyone can discover their God-given identity while fulfilling their God-ordained purpose. We do this through three different focuses: upreach (orienting ourselves towards God), inreach (connecting within our Wesley community and building relationships), and outreach (extending ourselves beyond the walls of the church to connect with the larger community through service and evangelism). We want to be a place where any student – regardless of their past or present – can be part of a community on campus. We are a ministry of the United Methodist Church but our students are from increasingly diverse faith backgrounds. Wherever you are coming from, you’re welcome here.

Wesley has many events that can vary from week to week, but there are two constants every single week:

  • On Sunday evenings at 6:00pm, we start our week with a prayer service along with communion.  This is a service led mainly by students sharing personal testimonies in an intimate environment.  Just as Jesus invited all to his table, we invite all to partake in communion along with the rest of the service.
  • On Wednesdays at 8:00pm we have our main service. Traditionally, this service is composed of three parts that function together: worship, a message, and small group discussion at the end.  It is a staple to our ministry and is a great way to connect with other students that attend Wesley.

Beyond that, we offer a variety of small groups where students can get connected to one another all while growing closer to God.  These groups range from doing activities such as playing games or sports to deeply reflecting on the way that Christ is working in one’s life in a smaller, discussion-based environment.  These groups form life-long friendships and are easy to get plugged into, just talk to someone here to get plugged in!

On top of all of that, we have other events throughout the year like retreats, pumpkin patch visits, Christmas dinner, spring banquet, a chili cookout. There’s always something going on.

We hope to see you soon. Roll Tide and God Bless.
summary: A Christian Community for all students that emphasizes faith, reason, and action in the historic tradition of Wesleyan theology. A ministry of the United Methodist Church.

name: Bama Ukirk

We invite you to learn more and consider how you might give life to the stories of unchurched youth in our community.

name: Medicine and Community
description: This organization is designed to develop social, personal, and professional skills in pre-health students through various service-learning projects and shadowing experiences in and around the Tuscaloosa area. Students will be introduced to various aspects of healthcare and issues, including different specialties, public health practices, and the process of becoming a professional in the world of health care.

name: Blue Key Honor Society
description:
Blue Key is a general honor society that bases its eligibility on all-around leadership in student life, high scholastic achievement, service to others, citizenship, and an adherence to principles of faith. 

Benefits of a Blue Key Membership:

· Recognition of scholastic, leadership, and service achievement on college campus.

· Registration as a lifetime member of a respected international honorary society.

· Acknowledge the contributions made by the top one percent of college students.

· Opportunity to engage in service activities with peers.

· Enhance leadership opportunities and experiences through organized interactions.
summary: Blue Key Honor Society is a premier honor society that recognizes seniors for balanced and all-around excellence in scholarship, leadership, and service.

name: The Association of Child Life
description: The Association of Child Life provides educational and volunteer opportunities, leadership experiences, and fellowship for University of Alabama Graduate and Undergraduate students interested in the profession of child life or the field of Human Development and Family Studies.
summary: ACL provides educational & volunteer opportunities, leadership experiences & fellowship for University of Alabama students interested in the profession of child life.

name: College of Communication and Information Sciences' Student Executive Council
description: The Student Executive Council (SEC) is made up of various College of Communication and Information Sciences (C&IS) leaders and exists to promote collaboration across C&IS student organizations, current students, faculty, staff, and administration. This is accomplished by developing and maintaining strong relationships with the C&IS departments to assist in identifying and implementing opportunities within the college, building strong relationships with faculty and staff and ensuring they direct student-related issues to the SEC, raising internal funds to support the C&IS Student Organization Fund, hosting college-wide events to encourage community, and overseeing student organization funding requests.
summary: The Student Executive Council exists to coordinate the organizations within the College; to foster stronger relationships between the students, faculty and alumni of the college; and to serve as a place for students to voice their concerns.

name: Alabama Aviation and Flying Club
description: We are here to expose students to the different types of aviation as well as different career fields in aviation. UA Aviaiton Association is going to explore military aviation, civilian, corporate, pistons, jets, experimental, and warbirds. We are here to help students explore aviation as a whole and not necessarily looking at it from a pilot's perspective, but also from a maintenance and business perpective.
summary: UA Aviation Association is a student organization meant to involve students in aviation while learning about all categories of aviation as well as the different possible careers in aviation.

name: Pre-Occupational Therapy Society
description: The purpose of the Pre-OT Society is as follows: to bring together undergraduate students at the University of Alabama interested in pursuing a career in occupational therapy; to aid students in preparing for graduate school; to reach out in the University of Alabama community and inform students on the career of occupational therapy.
summary: The Pre-OT Society brings together students interested in pursuing a Master's or Doctorate degree in Occupational Therapy and aids students in preparing for entry into the appropriate graduate school.

name: UA Meeting Professionals International
description: UAMPI is a chapter of Meetings Professional International at the University of Alabama. It provides students with opportunities to network with industry leaders, acquire high profile event industry experience, and stay connected with the ever-changing trends in events.
summary: UA MPI is a chapter of MPI at the University of Alabama. It provides students with opportunities to network,acquire high profile event industry experience, and stay connected with the trends within the event industry.

name: Iota Iota Iota-University of Alabama Chapter
description:
Iota Iota Iota (Triota): an honor society dedicated to the ideals of excellence in scholarship.

Triota promotes an interest in research, community service, and social issues. Triota strives to maintain feminist values central to women's studies: egalitarianism, inclusiveness, and diversity.
summary: Iota Iota Iota (Triota): an honor society dedicated to the ideals of excellence in women's studies scholarship; promotes an interest in research, community service, and social issues

name: The Pre-Physician Assistant Society
description:
PPAS is an organization focused on the overall preparation for prospective physician assistant students. Through our bi-monthly meetings, current physician assistants, program advisors, and PA students will provide superior information for the PA career and equip members for the application process. In addition, our meetings will provide an in depth view of the application process (CASPA) and how to prepare for the graduate record exam (GRE). We believe that through participation in PPAS, students will become more knowledgeable about the PA career and excel as future health care professionals. 

 

In addition, we provide our members with opportunities outside of meetings, including volunteer opportunities and social events so that our members have the ability to enhance their application with volunteer work and gain camaraderie with fellow PA students, resulting in long lasting friendships and resources for their journey as a Pre-PA student.
summary: PPAS is an organization focused on adequately providing students with the resources and knowledge they need to successfully apply and matriculate into Physician Assistant School after graduation.

name: UA Habitat for Humanity
description:
UA Habitat for Humanity is a student-run service organization with a mission to bring people together to build homes, communities, and hope. Our vision is a world where everyone has a decent place to live.

 

As a campus chapter, we partner with Habitat for Humanity Tuscaloosa to fulfill four functions: direct service, fundraising, advocating, and educating. During weekly Build Days, we help professional homebuilders construct homes for those in need. Additionally, we fundraise, host awareness events, and partner with campus organizations to further our mission. In 2021, we were awarded a $10,000 State Farm Youth Build Grant. To learn more about what we do, please visit our website.
summary: UA Habitat for Humanity is a student-run service organization with a mission to bring people together to build homes, communities, and hope. Our vision is a world where everyone has a decent place to live.

name: Sigma Tau Delta, Phi Xi Chapter
description:
We are the Phi Xi Chapter of the International English Honor Society Sigma Tau Delta. Sigma Tau Delta is open to majors in English, English Language or Arts Education, English/New College, and to minors in English or Creative Writing. Applicants must have completed 12 hours in English courses and have a 3.3 GPA in English and a 3.0 overall. The initiation fee is $65, and applications are available in English Building 103. 

Members have the opportunity to work on Dewpoint, our undergraduate literary journal, organize social events, participate in literacy-oriented volunteer opportunities, apply for and possibly attend Sigma Tau Delta's annual conference, and engage with the undergraduate literary community. 

You can contact President Maelee Hamlett for any questions or for more information.
summary: Phi Xi is the University of Alabama's chapter of Sigma Tau Delta, the International English Honor Society. We promote reading, writing, and publishing at the undergraduate level. We actively work to promote literacy in the Tuscaloosa community.

name: Alabama Astrobotics
description:
The Competition

The purpose of the competition is to design and build a robot to support life in space on the surface of the moon. The Moon's surface contains a buried material called icy regolith. This material contains water that can be harvested and used to support life on the moon. During the competition, our robot must traverse an arena that simulates the lunar surface, dig for gravel, and deposit it in a collection bin. In recent years the competitions focus has shifted to berm construction with the goal of building rocket launch and landing sites on the moon.

 

The Team

Alabama Astrobotics is divided into three main technical sub-teams along with administrative positions. The technical sub-teams are Mechanical, Electrical, and Software. The Mechanical team designs and builds the robot, Electrical handles all the wiring of the robot, and Software programs the robot to traverse the arena autonomously. Most of the members on our team are in the College of Engineering, but anyone can join the team. On the administration side we have positions in Marketing, Social Media, Budget, and Procurement. We also offer opportunities for people interested in technical writing and project management through our systems engineering team.

 

National Champions

The Astrobotics Team is highly decorated in awards from the annual competition. We have won first place nine times in the thirteen previous years of the competition, the last eight times in a row.

Championship Years: 

2024, 2022, 2021, 2019, 2018, 2017, 2016, 2015, 2012 

 

Outreach

In addition to building our robot, the team leads many outreach events both on the University's campus and in the surrounding area. We strive to share our love for space and robotics with younger students and encourage them to get involved in STEM.
summary: We are Alabama Astrobotics, a robotics team that competes each year in the NASA Lunabotics Competition at Kennedy Space Center. We also aim to increase STEM awareness in the community with various outreach events.

name: Bangladesh Student Association
description: The purpose of the Association is to introduce, celebrate, and promote the rich heritage, traditions, and cultural life of Bangladesh, while fostering a strong sense of community among Bangladeshi students at The University of Alabama. Rooted in the Bangladeshi values of hospitality, unity, and mutual support, the Association seeks to support both current and prospective Bangladeshi students by helping them navigate academic, social, and cultural life on campus. Through cultural programs, community gatherings, mentorship, and resource-sharing, the Association aims to preserve Bangladeshi identity abroad, create a welcoming home away from home, and ensure that every Bangladeshi student feels connected, supported, and empowered during their time at the University.
summary: The purpose of the Association shall be to introduce and to promote interest in all aspects of life and culture of Bangladesh and to serve the current and prospective students from Bangladesh studying at UA and to meet their various needs.

name: Student Involvement
description:
Student Involvement assists students in finding opportunities to be engaged and connect with the academic, local, and global community.
